toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

data.reasoningText:
The user wants to enable hot reload for their .NET test project. With `dotnet test`, hot reload is enabled via `dotnet watch test` or by setting the `DOTNET_WATCH` environment variable, but actually for `dotnet test` with the testing platform, hot reload can be enabled via `dotnet test --hot-reload` or by using `dotnet watch test`.

Actually, let me reconsider. The user is using `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport` which means they're using the newer Microsoft Testing Platform. For hot reload with `dotnet test`, the relevant setting is the `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` environment variable or command-line option `--hotreload`.
 
For the Microsoft Testing Platform, the simplest way to always have hot reload enabled without manually setting environment variables each time would be to configure it directly in the project file as an MSBuild property, or use the `--hotreload` flag with `dotnet test`. I can enable hot reload for `dotnet test` either via the `--hotreload` command-line flag or by setting the `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` environment variable, but to make it always enabled by default, I should add the `<TestingPlatformHotReload>true</TestingPlatformHotReload>` property to the project file. I'm going back and forth on the exact MSBuild property name here. The most reliable approach is to set `<TestingPlatformHotReload>true</TestingPlatformHotReload>` in the csproj PropertyGroup, though I'm not entirely certain that's the correct property—it might instead be `<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--hotreload</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>` or something similar based on how the Microsoft Testing Platform handles configuration. I'm settling on adding `<TestingPlatformHotReload>true</TestingPlatformHotReload>` to the csproj file as the most straightforward approach, and if that doesn't work I can explore environment variables or command-line alternatives. try it and see what happens. Let me add the property and then run `dotnet test` to verify.

Hmm, but actually, I want to be really sure about this. Let me look at what the actual property name is.

Looking at the Microsoft.Testing.Platform source and docs:
- The `dotnet test` integration with Testing Platform uses `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport` (which is already set)
- Hot reload is enabled with `--hotreload` flag
- The MSBuild property to always enable it would be... 

I think it might be `<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--hotreload</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>` but I'm not sure that's a real property.

OK let me try a different, more reliable approach. Since this is a .NET project, I could set an environment variable through the csproj or a runsettings file, but hot reload is really about the test runner process itself, not the test execution. So I should just check what `dotnet test --help` actually shows for hot reload options in this context.

arguments.description: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-0752c7bf3edc498f8138d63f8266ae93 && dotnet test --help 2>&1 | grep -i -A2 \"hot\" || true", "description": "Check dotnet test help for hot reload options", "initial_wait": 15}

toolArgs.description: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

data.reasoningText: The grep didn't find anything about hot reload. Let me check the full help output.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Description:
  .NET Test Driver

Usage:
  dotnet test [options] [[--] <additional arguments>...]]

Options:
  -s, --settings <SETTINGS_FILE>       The settings file to use when running tests.
  -t, --list-tests                     List the discovered tests instead of running the tests.
  -e, --environment <NAME="VALUE">     Sets the value of an environment variable. 
                                       Creates the variable if it does not exist, overrides if it does. 
                                       This will force the tests to be run in an isolated process. 
                                       This argument can be specified multiple times to provide multiple variables.
  
                                       Examples:
                                       -e VARIABLE=abc
                                       -e VARIABLE="value with spaces"
                                       -e VARIABLE="value;seperated with;semicolons"
                                       -e VAR1=abc -e VAR2=def -e VAR3=ghi
  --filter <EXPRESSION>                Run tests that match the given expression.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Run tests with priority set to 1: 
                                       --filter "Priority = 1"
                                                                               Run a test with the specified full name: 
                                       --filter "FullyQualifiedName=Namespace.ClassName.MethodName"
                                                                               Run tests that contain the specified 
                                       name: --filter "FullyQualifiedName~Namespace.Class"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-filtering for 
                                       more information on filtering support.
  --test-adapter-path <ADAPTER_PATH>   The path to the custom adapters to use for the test run.
  -l, --logger <LOGGER>                The logger to use for test results.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Log in trx format using a unique file 
                                       name: --logger trx
                                                                               Log in trx format using the specified 
                                       file name: --logger "trx;LogFileName=<TestResults.trx>"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-report for 
                                       more information on logger arguments.
  -o, --output <OUTPUT_DIR>            The output directory to place built artifacts in.
  --artifacts-path <ARTIFACTS_DIR>     The artifacts path. All output from the project, including build, publish, and 
                                       pack output, will go in subfolders under the specified path.
  -d, --diag <LOG_FILE>                Enable verbose logging to the specified file.
  --no-build                           Do not build the project before testing. Implies --no-restore.
  --results-directory <RESULTS_DIR>    The directory where the test results will be placed.
                                       The specified directory will be created if it does not exist.
  --collect <DATA_COLLECTOR_NAME>      The friendly name of the data collector to use for the test run.
                                                                               More info here: 
                                       https://aka.ms/vstest-collect
  --blame                              Runs the tests in blame mode. This option is helpful in isolating problematic 
                                       tests that cause the test host to crash or hang, but it does not create a memory 
                                       dump by default. 
  
                                       When a crash is detected, it creates an sequence file in 
                                       TestResults/guid/guid_Sequence.xml that captures the order of tests that were 
                                       run before the crash.
  
                                       Based on the additional settings, hang dump or crash dump can also be collected.
  
                                       Example: 
                                         Timeout the test run when test takes more than the default timeout of 1 hour, 
                                       and collect crash dump when the test host exits unexpectedly. 
                                         (Crash dumps require additional setup, see below.)
                                         dotnet test --blame-hang --blame-crash
                                       Example: 
                                         Timeout the test run when a test takes more than 20 minutes and collect hang 
                                       dump. 
                                         dotnet test --blame-hang-timeout 20min
  --blame-crash                        Runs the tests in blame mode and collects a crash dump when the test host exits 
                                       unexpectedly. This option depends on the version of .NET used, the type of 
                                       error, and the operating system.
  
                                       For exceptions in managed code, a dump will be automatically collected on .NET 
                                       5.0 and later versions. It will generate a dump for testhost or any child 
                                       process that also ran on .NET 5.0 and crashed. Crashes in native code will not 
                                       generate a dump. This option works on Windows, macOS, and Linux.
  
                                       Crash dumps in native code, or when targetting .NET Framework, or .NET Core 3.1 
                                       and earlier versions, can only be collected on Windows, by using Procdump. A 
                                       directory that contains procdump.exe and procdump64.exe must be in the PATH or 
                                       PROCDUMP_PATH environment variable.
<exited with exit code 0>

Description:

Usage:

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Description:
  
  
  Environment variables:
  
    DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER
    When set to '1' or 'true', dotnet-watch will poll the file system for
    changes. This is required for some file systems, such as network shares,
    Docker mounted volumes, and other virtual file systems.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH
    dotnet-watch sets this variable to '1' on all child processes launched.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH_ITERATION
    dotnet-watch sets this variable to '1' and increments by one each time
    a file is changed and the command is restarted.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH_SUPPRESS_EMOJIS
    When set to '1' or 'true', dotnet-watch will not show emojis in the 
    console output.
  
  Remarks:
    The special option '--' is used to delimit the end of the options and
    the beginning of arguments that will be passed to the child dotnet process.
  
    For example: dotnet watch -- --verbose run
  
    Even though '--verbose' is an option dotnet-watch supports, the use of '--'
    indicates that '--verbose' should be treated instead as an argument for
    dotnet-run.
  
  Examples:
    dotnet watch run
    dotnet watch test
  
      

Usage:
  dotnet-watch [options] [[--] <additional arguments>...]]
<exited with exit code 0>

    Docker mounted volumes, and other virtual file systems.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH
    dotnet-watch sets this variable to '1' on all child processes launched.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH_ITERATION
    dotnet-watch sets this variable to '1' and increments by one each time
    a file is changed and the command is restarted.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH_SUPPRESS_EMOJIS
    When set to '1' or 'true', dotnet-watch will not show emojis in the 
    console output.
  
  Remarks:
    The special option '--' is used to delimit the end of the options and
    the beginning of arguments that will be passed to the child dotnet process.
  
    For example: dotnet watch -- --verbose run
  
    Even though '--verbose' is an option dotnet-watch supports, the use of '--'
    indicates that '--verbose' should be treated instead as an argument for
    dotnet-run.
  
  Examples:
    dotnet watch run
    dotnet watch test
  
      

Usage:
  dotnet-watch [options] [[--] <additional arguments>...]]
<exited with exit code 0>

arguments.command: cd /tmp/sv-0752c7bf3edc498f8138d63f8266ae93 && dotnet test -- --help 2>&1 | grep -i -E "hot|reload" || echo "Not found"
arguments.description: Check testing platform help for hot reload

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Check testing platform help for hot reload

data.reasoningText:
So `dotnet watch test` is the way to get hot reload for tests. The user wants to avoid setting the `DOTNET_WATCH` environment variable every time. The `dotnet watch test` command automatically sets `DOTNET_WATCH=1` for child processes.

But the user is asking about enabling hot reload so it's "always enabled when I run from the command line." I think what they want is to be able to run `dotnet test` and have it automatically watch for changes and re-run, similar to what `dotnet watch test` does.

Actually, re-reading the question: "I want to set up hot reload for my test project so it's always enabled when I run from the command line. I don't want to set the environment variable every time."
 
They're probably using the Microsoft Testing Platform and want to configure hot reload at the project level rather than passing flags or environment variables each time. There might be a project property or configuration setting that enables this by default. I need to verify what properties the Microsoft Testing Platform actually supports for configuring hot reload in the project file, since I'm not entirely certain about the exact property name or whether `dotnet test` even has a `--hotreload` flag for this scenario. to see if it's recognized.

Hmm, but the user asked me to SET IT UP, not just tell them a command. Let me think about what the best project-level configuration is.

For .NET projects, the standard way to avoid setting environment variables every time is:

1. **`launchSettings.json`** - but this is for `dotnet run`, not `dotnet test`
2. **`.runsettings` file** - can set environment variables for test runs
3. **MSBuild properties in `.csproj`** - if there's a supported property
4. **`Directory.Build.props`** - same as above but at directory level

Since this is about `dotnet test`, the `.runsettings` approach could work if the env var controls the test host behavior.

But actually, the cleanest solution for "always run tests with hot reload" is to use `dotnet watch` as the default test runner, which could be configured through a script or project settings. Let me verify whether the testing framework actually supports the `--hotreload` flag.

data.reasoningText:
The project has compilation errors, but that's fine - that's not what I need to fix. The user asked about hot reload configuration.

For `dotnet watch test`, the way to make hot reload always enabled is through `dotnet watch`. The environment variable `DOTNET_WATCH` is automatically set by `dotnet watch`.

But the user said they don't want to set the environment variable every time. I think the best approach is to configure the project to use `dotnet watch` settings. Let me think about this differently.

Actually, re-reading the question again: "I want to set up hot reload for my test project so it's always enabled when I run from the command line. I don't want to set the environment variable every time."
 
I'm realizing the user probably wants a persistent configuration rather than having to remember to use `dotnet watch test` each time. For .NET 9, I should check what project-level settings are available for hot reload behavior, or whether they need to create a `.dotnetcli.json` or similar configuration file to make this the default behavior. I'm second-guessing whether those MSBuild properties actually exist in the current .NET version. Let me refocus on what the user actually wants: they need `dotnet test` to run with hot reload enabled by default from the command line, without manually setting environment variables each time. The most straightforward solution is probably using `dotnet watch test` with a configuration in the project file, or checking if there's a direct property for the Testing Platform itself. their normal workflow) to automatically have hot reload enabled. The environment variable they're probably setting is `DOTNET_WATCH_SUPPRESS_STATIC_FILE_HANDLING` or `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED`.

Actually, for the Microsoft Testing Platform (MSTest 3.x with `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport`), hot reload during `dotnet test` is a feature that can be enabled. Looking at MSTest 3.x documentation, the `--hotreload` option is available when running tests directly through the testing platform. When using `dotnet test` with `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport`, additional arguments can be passed after `--`, so `dotnet test -- --hotreload` might work if the version supports it.

To make it always enabled, I'd need to set a property in the project file or configure it through a `.testingplatform` configuration file or MSBuild properties. Let me think through the most practical approach—checking what environment variable the user might be referring to and finding the best way to make it permanent. For `dotnet watch test`, the `DOTNET_WATCH` environment variable is set automatically, so the user shouldn't need to set it manually. For Microsoft Testing Platform hot reload, the environment variable might be `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` or similar. The cleanest solution is probably adding configuration through a `Directory.Build.props` file, modifying the `.csproj` directly, or using a `.runsettings` file. I need to clarify what "hot reload for tests" actually means—either file watching with automatic re-runs (what `dotnet watch test` does) or true code patching without restarting. For tests, the first interpretation is more practical, but `dotnet test` itself can't be changed to `dotnet watch test`. Instead, I can configure the `dotnet watch` behavior in the project file to ensure hot reload works properly when someone does use `dotnet watch`.

Now I'm thinking through what environment variables or MSBuild properties might control this behavior—things like `DOTNET_WATCH_RESTART_ON_RUDE_EDIT` that affects how `dotnet watch` handles certain code changes. In .NET 9 with the Testing Platform, there might be specific configuration options in MSTest 3.x that let you set up this watch-and-rerun behavior directly in the project file. I'm reconsidering the approach - setting an environment variable through `Directory.Build.props` seems like the cleanest way to make this work consistently across the project without requiring manual setup or shell scripts. Build properties don't set environment variables for the process. They're just MSBuild variables.

To set an environment variable during `dotnet test`, we'd need the test runner to pick it up. The `.runsettings` file can do this.

Actually, I just realized something. For the Microsoft Testing Platform (MSTest 3.x), there IS a way to configure extensions and options via MSBuild properties. Looking at the MSTest source code, the hot reload extension can be configured via:

```xml
<EnableMicrosoftTestingPlatformHotReload>true</EnableMicrosoftTestingPlatformHotReload>
```

or

```xml
<TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled>true</TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled>
```

But I'm not confident about the exact property name.

Let me go with the approach I'm most confident will work: adding environment variables to the `.runsettings` file. Though I'm realizing I still don't actually know which environment variable the user is trying to set.

Let me try a different approach. I can add a property to the csproj to enable hot reload for the testing platform. Actually, `dotnet watch test` is the standard way to get hot reload for tests in .NET, and I can configure watch-specific settings in the project file to make it the default behavior.

Configuring watch settings... I'm realizing that enabling hot reload directly in `dotnet test` without the `watch` command isn't straightforward through MSBuild properties alone—the testing platform's CLI flag is the standard approach. Let me focus on what's actually configurable: `dotnet watch test` already has hot reload by default, and for always-on hot reload with plain `dotnet test`, the user would need to use the `--hotreload` flag or create a custom script.
